Reports and Correspondence between The
Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's
Navy and James Sowerby F.L.S. &c.

1812

M^r. Lukin's Comp^ts to M^r. Sowerby
will be glad to know what time it will be
convenient to him to leave London.

Somers Hill
July 18^th. 1812

[Address panel of a folded letter]

M^r. Sowerby

[Red wax seal]

REPORT

and

Correspondance

between

The Honourable Commissioners

of

[Archival stamp: BRITISH MUSEUM (NAT. HIST.)]

His Majesty's Navy

(and)

James Sowerby F.L.S.

(1812)

Navy Office 15^th July 1812

Sir

I have the commands of the Commissioners of the Navy to request you will do them the favor to attend at this Office tomor-row at three O'clock.

I am, Sir, Your very humble Servant
R. C. Nelson

Sowerby Esq^r 2 Mead Place, Lambeth; near the Asylum.

---

Navy Office 17^th July 1812

Sir

We desire you will proceed with M^r Lionel Lukin to Plymouth, as early as possible, & inspect the state of His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte, which has recieved very considerable injury by the Dry rot, with a view to ascertain what measures it may be advisable to adopt to stop its further progress if possible, by preventing the vegetation & spreading of the Fungi.

We further desire that your report on this subject may be extended to the measures necessary to prevent this evil in any of His Majesty's Ships; and that you will make it either in conjunction with M^r Lukin or separately, as you may find most convenient.

We are, Sir

Your humble Servants
F. T. Hartwell
H. Legge
T. B. Thompson.

Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place
Lambeth.

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

Shocked at the report of the rotting state of many of our National Bulwarks I felt ready to make enquiry as to the probability of curing or preventing the evil; when, in consequence of my known experience among subjects which thrive under the same circum-stances as those which produce and develope the decay of Timber, viz. Fungi, I happened to be called upon for that purpose; & I there-fore felt it an imperative duty, notwithstanding my other necessary occupations to attend immediately to the subject, & not less so when I percieved the penetration and anxiety of your Honourable Board by the questions you asked me; my answers to which, when in any degree to the purpose were treasured up in a manner that convinced me how very desirous you were to execute with fidelity your most arduous trust.

Desirous of doing the best I wished to have substi-tuted some one of more general information & so more capable of the enquiry, but as I knew of nobody who had condescended to study sufficiently the habits of Fungi, I had no alternative. Therefore, while I might feel proud, in a concern of such magnitude of the hopes of being able to do good, I could only consider myself as an instrument in the hands of Providence selected, for a particular department at a crisis so momentous as the present.

In many cases there is but one way to do right & this is often hard to find, but sometimes seems so easy that every one is confident of success; this confidence, however, is often delusive as appears in respect to the Dry-rot, the cause of which,

Confined Damp, is so palpable that it might be thought easy to remove; yet, from the day of King Charles to the present time of increased experience & improvement, its ravages have become more and more distressing.

The confidence I feel of proposing a means of eradicating & preventing this now so peculiarly unfortunate evil arises from an experience that no one else that I know, has had so long an opportunity of obtaining. The minutiae of my observations are very tedious and altogether difficult for me to detail, even to persons of much experience, without some danger of mistakes that might lead to much mischief. I have seen instances of salutary advice misapplied through a wrong or [text crossed out: perfect] imperfect conception of the principles of it; I hope, therefore, to have time allowed for a fuller report, but since the immediate object of my enquiry, the probability of repairing His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte which appears to me capable of being repaired, the delay of a day is truly alarming, I shall now suggest those means Nature has pointed out to me as promising the greatest security, happily at but little or no additional expence.

I first saw His Majesty's Ship the Queen Charlotte on the 24^th of July & again on the 25^th I found that great quantities of Timber had been removed from her & that it was

*Methods have often been devised & put into execution which have actually nourished the evil instead of removing it; instances of which are recent & well known.

† The Foudroyant of 80 Guns built at Plymouth in 1798 & the Ajax, built by contract in 1798 were nearly destroyed by Fungi in the space of about three years.

intended still to take out more, which appeared to be proper; the lower decks & hold were very close, requiring air; there were more or less vestiges of Fungi from the middle deck downwards; they were of the following species, Boletus hybridus C.F. tab. 289, being the young state of Boletus medulla panis E.F. 326. (White rugged Soft Fungi) Xylostroma giganteum (A Gigantic Leather-like Fungus peculiar to rotten Oak) E.F. 358. Auricularia pulverulenta E.F. 214 & some others of but little consequence.

[in left margin: A knowledge of the habits of Fungi necessary for the purpose] The various habits of these different species would, if attended to, point out the proper cautions necessary to prevent their growth. There were very little remains of Boletus Lachrymans E.F. 113, the best known Dry rot.

[in left margin: Hygrometer recommended.] On the morning of the 25^th an Hygrometer was left for an hour near the pump well in the Hold; it indicated a very damp air standing at 828.6° outside the ship it had stood at 404.2° perfect wetness being indicated by 1000°. I should recommend the general use of this instrument as likely to point out the state of air favourable for the growth of Fungi. I have found by experiment that wood will rot & Fungi grow at 800° or 900°.

I have ventured to declare that the Queen Charlotte may be repaired, because I have hopes that the further progress of vegetation may be stopped, but unusual caution is requisite with her from the circumstance of the Timber of which she is built being in a state fit for vegetation whenever it is placed in a favourable situation.

[in left margin: A free current of air to be admitted.] In the first place I should recommend the removal of every obstruction to a free current of air through and around every part possible and stretching an awning over the whole ship at some distance above the deck and so placed as to keep

off rain without dripping upon the vessel & at the same time to admit a current of air beneath it; the awnings at present in use are not sufficiently extensive, they are pervious to rain & lie so close to the deck that there is some reason to fear that by confining moisture they may promote destructive fermentation, the immediate cause of the rot, which is the more probable in the present favourable season for the growth of Fungi.

[in left margin: A current of air necessary] Thus, by a current of air I would endeavour to remove all moisture & so leave no nourishment for the parasite; [in left margin: A person should be appointed to see these precautions used] I would even suggest the appointment of some able person to see the various parts as thoroughly dried as possible, by air, before any fresh wood is put in.

[in left margin: Artificial heat objectionable. Ventilation. Cold air may be used to dry. M^r. Lukin's ventilation.] I should object to the use of artificial heat as likely to increase the temperature of the moisture & consequently the fermentation without removing it, but a rapid draught of cold air which may be caused by stoves so placed as not to heat the Timber, will be useful to dry such parts as the air can otherwise scarcely have access to; this kind of ventilation M^r. Lukin has already employed with success.

[in left margin: The best seasoned Timber to be used. The ship to be kept dry.] I would next suggest the necessity of using the best seasoned Timber in the repair and to keep this as dry as possible by laying it dry and open and raised from the ground near the workmen and should any part of the ship have got wet by any means it will be proper to let it dry before fresh wood is placed over it; [in left margin: No fresh wood to be placed on a wet part. No hollows to be filled with shavings or dirt.] and care should be taken that no hollows are covered up that contain shavings, sawdust or any dirt capable of retaining moisture.

[in left margin: Boiled planks] As it appears necessary to put the boiled planks on while moist it will be proper to use every endeavour to let the planks have air enough to dry internally, thus they may be put on alternately, leaving spaces to be filled by intermediate

[Page number '10' in top right corner]

ones after the first set have had sufficient time to dry; two or three planks may be kept off 'till all the others are supposed to be thoroughly dry that when the whole is closed, as little moisture as possible may be confined. It will also be requisite, at this season of the year, particularly, not to paint any part that is not previously as dry as possible, so that the oil may rather penetrate the wood itself, than drive moisture before it.

I concieve that any impregnation of the wood is unnecessary, if the wood be properly dried; and many acid and or saline impregnations I have heard recommended as perfect cures, are certainly detrimental to the wood, bolts & workmen.

The Pitch Pine that is to be replaced should be perfectly cleared of every vestige of decay and exposed in an open situation at a distance from the ground & allowed to be thoroughly dried be-fore it is used again. I need not insist upon the propriety of re-moving all Timber that is so rotten as to be useless.

These, seemingly trifling preliminaries, if not understood clearly, may be carried with honest ardour to such extremes as to contradict one another, or through inattention to minutiae may be so executed as not to answer the intended purpose and I feel so highly concerned that I dread any misapplication.

As the assiduous, but misguided endeavours of some to do good will establish the foundation of this fear, I shall take the liberty to repeat an instance I observed on board the Queen Char-lotte, and add a circumstance or two which have occurred on board another of His Majesty's Ships: 1^st in endeavouring to keep off rain coverings have been laid so close to the Deck as to encourage vegetation by enclosing wet that had accidentally got through some holes, in the same way as, if on the ground floor of my

[Page number '11' in top right corner]

house on a level with Land springs I leave a Board or piece of Moor Cloth for a few days the Dryrot is sure to grow under it. On board another Ship, the San Domingo, with the laudable design of preventing the fall of chips, rain, rubbish &c into several hollows, these hollows have been covered over without clearing away what had already got in, thus securing the damp stagnated air with the mischief it was nursing, which, if increased, will of course have its concomitant evils & besides various species of Fungi, their attendant insects, as appears particularly on board this Ship, where the Oniscus asellus of Linneus or Common Hoglouse has become so abundant as to remind us of the plagues of Egypt & spread abroad so much as even to appear at the Officers meals: On board this same Ship much proper attention has been paid to the admission of air by means of Lattice work, yet I noticed with regret that many of the obscure parts of the vessel were defective in that kind of cleanliness necesary to keep her moderately dry and disturb the haunts of Insects.

I have been extremely anxious to make this report as soon as possible; I could have made several observations relating to the mode of stacking the Timber intended for immediate use; of preserving it after being wrought &c, but I was unwilling to enter into the latter part of my examination without more infor-mation, fearing I might say too little or too much to be intelligible.

The haste with which I have drawn up these obser-vations relating to the Queen Charlotte, added to the circumstance of its being the first time of my making an Official Report, I beg to plead as an excuse for such inaccuracies or omissions as may occur.

I did not join M^r Lukin on a report, as I had

[Page number '12' in top right corner]

reason to suppose that what he would have said would only have been a repetition of former reports, of which he told me he had given many; and I was desirous of giving my individual opinion, that, should it be found consistent with other reports it might be acted upon with greater confidence, but if it differed, the experience of the Board would enable them to judge impartially.

I must now express my gratitude to M^r Lukin for the assistance he has given me & his assiduity in pointing out what most nearly related to the subject. Among other things the circumstance of the hollows containing rubbish being covered up, [marginal note in left margin: board the Domingo.] is one, for the knowledge of which I am particularly indebted to him.

I am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obed^t Servant
Ja^s Sowerby

1^th August 1812.

Navy Office 28^th August 1812

Mr Sowerby

Having furnished the Lords Commissioners
of the Admiralty with a Copy of your report dated the 18^th
instant relative to the measures necessary to be adopted to prevent
the progress of the Dryrot in His Majesty's Ship Queen Charlotte,
their Lordships have been pleased to direct that the means which
you have recommended, shall be carried into execution in repairing
that Ship as well as in building & repairing His Majesty's
Ships at the several Yards; And as Hygrometers are in conse-
quence to be used generally, we request that you will state to us
the names and address of the makers of those instruments whose
construction you prefer.

It is intended to print such parts of your report
as apply to the preservation of Ships, in order that they may
be circulated among the Officers of the several Yards, but we
request you will inform us whether you have any further ob-
servations to offer on the subject, in order that the publication
may be complete.

We have now the pleasure to mention that we
are directed by the Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty
to state to you that they consider you entitled to their thanks
and approbation for your attention and Report.

We are

Your affectionate Friends,

F. T. Hartwell

H. Legge

T. B. Thompson

Navy Office, 26^th Jan^y 1813

Sir

We enclose you the copy of a letter dated the 7^th instant
from M^r. M. Parker of the Little Carron Colour works at Leith,
stating that he has discovered the means of for preventing the Dryrot
in Timber; and request you to favour us with your opinion
thereon.

We are

Sir

Your humble Servants,

F. T. Hartwell

Will^m Rule

H. Legge.

Ja^s. Sowerby Esq^r
Mead Place, Lambeth

Copy)

"Gen^t." the Dryrot in Timber Ships having lately become
a great national Loss, besides an incalculable disappointment, it
therefore becomes an object of more than common importance, to
hit upon some mode of preventing the same; Imagination some-
times leads us to attempt the performance of impossibilities; and
an attempt to eradicate or prevent this evil may be considered
futile or purely imagenable, having hitherto baffled the most inge-
nious, yet it may not be impossible, and I trust my present
ideas will not appear to be the result of illguided imagination
and I feel assured that any attempt, likely to attain so desirable
a purpose will not be discarded by you. _

The Dryrot in Ships is universally admitted to be
caused by an Insect; if so, the remedy will not oppose such diffi-

-culty, but on the contrary, should it be a natural defect of the wood
or occasioned by the partial exclusion of atmospheric air, or some other
cause we cannot immediately account for, still it does not
argue the same cannot be prevented. I never have seen
a Ship in this situation, but I have repeatedly seen Oak
Timber in various situations completely mouldered away,
except in some particular spots, and I presume from the
same cause as in Ships; and in these instances my attention
has been drawn to the part which appeared in the decayed
mass perfect; and in such case it has been the object of my
pursuit to find out the cause of such partial preservation, &
by which, I flatter myself, I have found out the means
at a comparative small expence, to eradicately prevent the
same.

Having had the honour to correspond with you
on a former occasion, I thought it proper to make the present
communication to you, feeling assured you will give the matter
that attention which its importance deserves. _ if it should be
found necessary I could wait upon you in order to explain
the means I propose, and on which I shall be glad
of the honour of a reply.

I am &c &c

I. M. Parker.

Little Carron Colour Works

Leith 7^th Jan^y 1813

The Hon^ble Commissioners of
His Majesty's Navy

The Experiments I have made on wood far advanced in the Dryrot
has been universally successful.

If any reference should be necessary, I beg to mention that I am
a relation of M^r. Madgshon late one of the Masters of Chatham Yard

To the Honourable Commissioners of H.M. Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

I have received your letter requesting my opinion on Mr. J. M. Packers stating his having discovered the means of preventing the [text crossed out: means of prev] dryrot in Timber. You, of course, must be somewhat aware of the delicacy of my situation & see that to give an opinion upon his Ideas is as difficult as to interpret Nebuchadnezzars dream. Conscious of your previous information and judgement I venture to presume that you did not before understand the Dryrot in Ships as unanimously admitted to be caused by an Insect. This, and what follows leads me to suppose you are well aware how much this Gentleman is likely properly to understand of this serious concern.

I am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your Obedient humble Servant

Jas. Sowerby.

(28 January 1815

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen.

It is with great pleasure that I find by your letter of the 28^th August that my report has met with the appro-bation of My Lords Commissioners of the Admiralty. I feel most highly gratified by their Lordships thanks.

I ought not to have let so much time elapse but have done myself the pleasure to reply sooner; the delay, is, however, fortunate, for whatever confidence I had of success, arising from a continual watchfulness over the habits of Fungi for many years, yet to others I could not but consider it as a new subject and very convenient to have some sort of evidence of probable success for future encouragement, which, from pre-vious experience I felt the more desirous of, as the concern is of so much magnitude that more hasty conclusions might have been liable to censure for want of that proper and favourable evidence that may now, I hope, be fairly admitted; besides, I have had an opportunity of considering my report, of collecting my Ideas into a regular & concise plan for operation and of making more numerous observations & further con-firming by them what appears to me the simplest & most cer-tain means of accomplishing so desirable an end.

As their Lordships have been pleased to direct that the means I have had the honour to recommend to check the ravages of the Dryrot on board His Majesty's Ship the Queen Charlotte should be carried strictly into execution.

* I cannot but express that I am deeply sensible of the honour their Lordships have done me in sending S^r W^m Rule & M^r Knowles

sincerely hope they will prove successful, but it must be expected that a ship which has suffered so much can never be rendered quite so sound as a ship built at first with proper materials & due precaution.

[marginalia in left margin, pencil: any material circumstance relating to the Queen Charlotte]
I am not aware that I have omitted any material circumstance relating to that ship and I believe that by applying the same principles in finishing any other Ship as your Honourable Board have been pleased to suggest, much may be done towards the total prevention of the rot. Should the operations I have recommended for the Queen Charlotte continue to be attended with the success I anticipated, they will give confidence, exciting more particular attentions & becoming a powerful stimulus to further exertion.

In the mean time, as your Honourable Board "further require that my report may be extended to the measures "necessary to prevent the evil in any of His Majesty's Ships" I beg leave to offer the following observations relating to the seasoning of Timber, a subject, upon the discussion of which for want of time I had not entered in my former report.

[marginalia in left margin, pencil: The Timber to be carefully felled & if free from internal Fungi & rot to be seasoned as soon as felled without artificial heat, sheltered and supported from the ground upon which they should never afterw be suffered to lie quiet long]
The trees, having been felled at a proper time of year and upon examination found to be free from internal Fungi & rot * the seasoning should be immediately commenced and carried on regularly until the last moment of fitting together, without artificial heat; they should even be sheltered from rain & the heat of the sun & supported from the ground upon which they should never afterw be suffered to lie quiet long; with my report to Plymouth, since in their experienced hands I have the satisfaction to learn it has been successful in a case rendered more desperate by the mistakes that had preceded my report.

* An externally good looking lining Oak has been so full of the rot as to

they should be kept separate from one another at all times, especia^lly
when not quite dry and if brought from distant Countries by sea,
they should be put on Shipboard as dry as possible & not stowed
too close, for incipient fermentation is much to be feared, espe-
cially if the Timber be fresh cut, it destroys the connection of the
fibres and is difficult to check.

*[In left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
Stacks of Timber

The Timbers having been squared & reduced to a
certain state of dryneß should be stacked upon high ground, the
lowest ones being raised some feet from the green earth; the next
supported at a proper distance, perhaps twice the measure of its
thickneß and those nearest to the top of the stack may be closest.
The distance of the Timbers from each other may vary with the situa-
tion of the Stacks and the state of the wood, those most confined, in
the lowest ground or containing freshest wood being most open,

*[In left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
free acceß of proper air necessary.

since the free acceß of proper air not only prevents fermentation
and the growth of Fungi, but tends to harden the wood & so renders
it leß liable to be injured by damp hereafter and more unfit for
the nourishment of the Dryrot.

*[In left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
High stacks better than long ones.
Stacks to be so ranged as not to prevent the air pervading the openings.

High stacks are better than long ranges,
particularly in low confined marshy Countries, or adjacent
to our coast; and one stack should never be placed so as
to prevent the wind from entering the open spaces of
another.

*[In left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
Damp from Fungi, Timber turned & fresh stacked.

Should the wood show any signs of
Fungi, which, notwithstanding much caution, may sometimes
happen when the Timber has been placed too close, stacked
too early, or from a succession of close, damp weather,
it must be cleaned & occasionally turned & fresh stacked.
Lest it be easily crumbled to pieces: it is proper to observe if there be any
infant signs of this before using it.

It will be found generally useful to pull down
the stacks after a short time and build them afresh nearer the
place where the Timber is to be used, the upper Timbers being
brought to the bottom of the fresh stack, the top of which will
be formed by those that were at the bottom of the old stack, this
movement being repeated several times will facilitate & promote
the healthy seasoning of the wood, & at the same time bring it
nearer to the hands of the builder. In this way of proceeding
the time required to remove Timber from the place where it was
felled may be so economised as to advance the seasoning very
considerably; constantly recollecting to keep it from rain & the
wet ground; thus in many cases much time may be gained. If
Timber cut at different periods be placed in the same stack it
will not be difficult to regulate the turning of the stacks so as
to bring that which is most seasoned to the workmens hands
first. The wood that is removed for the use of the builders

*[In left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
Timber should be kept under cover, the seasoning will still go on

should, all the time it is in their hands, be kept under shelter
from rain even when worked into the frame, and as high as
possible in the stocks with a free passage for air around the
very keel.

These proceedings being attended to, the Timber
may be considered as seasoning more & more until the Ship
It might be of advantage if some smart arrangement could be

*[In a different hand, in left margin, next to the paragraph below]*
Stacks may be ranged ornamentally.

made for order & convenience in placing the wood according
to its most appropriate purposes, ornamentally imitative of the
Muskets in an armoury. It would become the boast of some in-
dividuals, whose skill is naturally adapted to it, and at length
the pride of the nation to contemplate on a neat & cleanly ar-
rangement, pleasing in the present & offering a fair prospect for
the future.

is finished, when she need not remain in Dock or Ordinary for
seasoning and if then kept clean & dry according to observations
in a former report she may still be improving the quality of her
Timbers, getting the better for wear and absolutely increasing in value
for a long time, even so as at the end of another century to vie
with the Royal William instead of requiring to be almost rebuilt
in a few years. By this mode of seasoning there will always be
wood free from rot or Fungi & fit for building durable Ships:
the Timber yards also being kept clean, all the repositories will
have a more comfortable pleasant & wholesome appearance.

I do not doubt, after the late prevailing unfortunate
circumstances that the wisdom of your Honourable Board will
contrive that every requisite attention shall be paid to the subject &
will also determine upon the means to carry so great a concern into
execution in the most proper manner.

As "Hygrometers are to be used generally and
as I am requested to state the names & addresses of the makers
of the instruments whose construction I prefer; I beg leave to
state that it was my desire to get an Hygrometer constructed of
a different form from the one I had used and which might be
easier applied, but I have not yet got it completed. Should I
obtain one that I think will answer I will submit it to the
inspection of your Honourable Board. The one I used is called

*[In left margin, next to the line below]*
Kater's Hygrometer

Kater's Hygrometer, it was made by M^r Jones of Oxendon Street
It answers the purpose extremely well, but its divisions require

*[In left margin, next to the line below]*
Divisions require calculation to be calculated to...

to be calculated to bring them to a common standard, & each
instrument requires a different calculation. Any other in
which the extremes of wet and dry are marked, may do as D^r
Luc's &c. Immersion in water or perfect wetneß is considered

as 1000^o above perfect dryness.
When the Index is below 300^o the air is so dry
as to be uncomfortable & injurious to Timber, cracking & opening it
as in hot climates. From 400^o to 600^o in our vicinity I have found
agreable & safe, above that point the air is sensibly damp &
more especially if confined and rather warm, supports the
growth of Fungi: From 800^o to 900^o is very dangerous in any moderate
temperature, often causing the rot in Timber & promoting the growth
of Fungi, in all cases the danger is augmented by stagnation of
the moist air or moisture & increase of temperature. I have seen
the Lycoperdon carpobolus (of Linneus & Othungi tab. 224 bisch Fungus
that discharges a sort of capsule to a considerable distance like a shell
from a mortar) grow on rotten damp wood both when exposed
to frost out of doors and in a pure heat in the late Duke of
Portlands hot house at Bulstrode. There are few Fungi that
will grow upon wood that is covered by water although that
wood may be in a state of rapid decay.
I have before insisted upon the propriety
of avoiding all dark holes or corners where damp may get
confined or where there is any difficulty in cleaning; I would
in addition recommend that to discover the earliest indications
of the rot the ship should be often cleaned & all suspicious
parts examined with the Hygrometer. No place should be
allowed to remain long without fresh air being introduced.
If the Hygrometer stands above 600^o, if it be above 700^o the air
should be often changed or a current should be passed through
or over the place until the Hygrometer return to the same degree
as is indicated by it in the open air. If this operation be per-
formed in dry weather it will be most effectual. The instrument

should be allowed to remain a quarter of an hour in the neigh-
bourhood of the most suspected wood & the appointed Officers
should pay especial attention to the middle & lower parts and
dark recesses or corners of Ships
There is no doubt but that Oak and Mahogany,
some parts of which I understand are now cheap, are good ma-
terials for the large & strong parts of ships where Iron is not
convenient; Oak, more especially English, may become nearly
as hard as Iron by age and proper attention. Deal of any kind
is always comparatively soft and spongy, whatever precaution
may be taken, and though it may be strong enough for some purposes,
it is very apt to absorb water so should be as much avoided as possible
but most carefully where it is likely to be covered over (Its being
splintery renders it an unmerciful act to use more than can be
helped in Ships of war. I think also that the crowding many
pieces together in place of one larger one is a bad thing.
Every Englishman must feel grateful to your
Honourable Board for the pains you have taken in your arduous
trust. With the best hope that your endeavours may be crowned
with the most brilliant success I remain
Honourable Gentlemen
Your very faithful & humble Servant
Ja^s Sowerby

2 Mead Place Lambeth 17^th Feb^y 1813.

* It would be proper also to make allowance for the temperature in determining
the degree of moisture (for the higher the temperature the lower the dangerous
point on the instrument) for which tables may be constructed as soon as
the kind of Hygrometer best adapted to the purpose is determined on.

Navy Office 12^th March 1813

Sir

With reference to your report of the 17^th ultimo relative to
the Dryrot in Timber;— we request you will state to us the
names and address of the makers of Hygrometers which you
would recommend to be used, corresponding with the calculations
of wetness and different degrees of moisture as enumerated in
that Report.

We are, Sir, Your humble Servants
F. T. Hartwell
W^m Rule
H: Legge

Ja^s Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place Lambeth.

---

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen
24^th March 1813

According to your desire with reference to my
Report of the 17^th ult^o I can only say at present that M^r Jones
of Oxendon Street, Mathematical Instrument maker, who was a
pupil of Ramsdens, made the instrument I used, and at my
desire is about to make some to revolve 10 times, equal to 1000^o
parts, (instead of odd numbers) which will save the trouble of cal-
culation and agree with the trials by which I calculated the wetness
that is mischievous. I waited thus long expecting to see one every
day.
De Luc's instrument is to be had of M^r Hausmann
of Cumberland Lodge, Windsor Great Park with improvements &
the calculations relating to the Thermometer, which, when printed
may render his instrument preferable in the opinions of some per-
sons.
I am Honourable Gentlemen, Your very obed^t
humble Servant Ja^s Sowerby

Navy Office 16^th October 1813

Sir,

With reference to your Reports on the premature
decay of His Majesty's Ships, in which you have made several
observations with respect to the mode of stowing Timber, We request
that you will attend one of the Surveyors of the Navy to Deptford
& Woolwich Dock Yards any Morning in the early part of next
week, in order that you may see the mode of stowing Timber in
those Dock yards, and point out how far your suggestions can
be carried into execution.

We are

Sir

Your humble Servants

F. T. Hartwell.

R. Seppings

G. Stewart.

Jas. Sowerby Esq^r
2 Mead Place
Lambeth

To the Honourable the Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy

Honourable Gentlemen

That you have not forgotten my reports on
the premature decay of His Majesty's Ships is an high gratification
to me and it flatters my feelings to the utmost to know that they
have so long and so continually been under consideration, being
a proof of the pains your Honourable Board takes in a concern
confessedly of the first National Importance. Permit me to presume
that they have tended to increase the care taken to keep His
Majesty's Ship the Lord Nelson judiciously and sweetly aired,
by making use of the advantages Nature presents in the best way
in her present position; keeping her in a medium state, neither
too dry nor too wet; and, May I not hope that my Reports
will be continually attended to, because it is easy to follow their
directions and they may in time be regulated so as to produce
the best effects.

I was very much pleased with the zeal of
M^r Seppings, his penetration & extensive practical knowledge
stamps great value on his judgement, and I was happy to
answer his very judicious & instructive queries in every case as
far as in my power.

Attending more particularly according to your
desire dated the 16^th ins^t. "to see the mode of stowing Timber &
point out how far my suggestions can be carried into execution"
[text crossed out: The fences to overcome Timber well arrangd] I cannot but regret that there should appear so much difficulty,
which M^r Seppings has pointed out; yet would I cherish
a hope, that for the credit of my country & all concerned, a
litle more will be done towards raising from the ground, arrang-

and clearing round the stacks of Timber; and as a litle improvement
leads to greater, so, in the end, seeing plainer the utility promised
we may arrive at the desired perfection. That the Timber may be
better stowed is most certain, and perhaps room may be gained
by it, but whether sufficient for the desired supply is doubtful:
it is always healthy to have plenty of room, confined places are
always detrimental to everything. This thing and indeed all others
as I hinted in my second report might be executed by some one
or more among so many who might be chosen as most adequate
for such a purpose; for in any line there are some persons more
particularly efficient than others, and I would willingly assist,
if required. I think, at least in marshy places (as in the new
[text crossed out: places / platforms / of stone] ground at Deptford, where the Timber is lying in a very uncom-
fortable & hazardous manner) paved platforms should be
raised with arches, stones or other supports to keep the Timber
from the ground and open beneath; as few horizontal plains
as possible must be contrived to those supports, always allowing
in every part an inclining plain to prevent any lodgement
for water or damps. The stacks should be in the proportion of a
cube or about as high as they are long and wide. Each stack
may support a moveable shelter or covering to defend it from
the sun and rain, but admitting air by open lathing boards,
it might also support side portable lathing boards or shelters construct-
ed on a principle to open or shut as occasion requires. Thus might
the Stacks be kept in a tolerably regular medium according
with the Hygrometer, neither liable to crack from the heat of
the sun or to be wetted by the admission of rain. The platforms
might be contrived of proportions adapted to the different
sorts, sizes, or intended purposes to which the Timbers are to be

put and arranged in such order that an immediate reference may
be had to the particular kinds and calculations made of the relative
proportions of each kind, which would give neatness without confusion
and admits of elegance. Of these things you Gentlemen will be
best judges. A certain expenditure is often more laudable than
extravagant as it not only commands attention, but ease & respect;
and, which is essential in this particular, a dislike for litter and
partiality for cleanliness.

If a model were to be made, it might be better
understood and amended; and I believe, with the little notion I
have of drawing I could direct it if you require. I have the
less hesitation in this recommendation, as it is the safer way with
less probable expence by much than any other endeavor to pre-
serve wood I am aquainted with.

No doubt, I presume can now be entertained of
the use of wood, which has from the remotest ages been given &
recommended as suitable to floating bodies; and its admirable durability
[marginal note in left margin, very faint: naturally?] when well managed, either by chance or by proper attention, needs
no comment, and even its durability under a variety of casualties
is very astonishing; its goodness has occasionally thus become a
misfortune by encouraging schemes which have by experience
been too often found fatal. I cannot, with conscious integrity
do other than recommend an early and careful attention to the
[marginal note in left margin: Large Timbers to be selected first] selecting and distinguishing the best wood & preserving such more
[marginal note in left margin: selected Spars] particularly from imbibing any destructive principle, in order
that a choice may be at hand for any necessary purpose. It
will be another way of beginning even a salutary thing in the
more crowded Dock Yard at Deptford & in that less crowded at
Woolwich, and also not difficult to accomplish according to the

slight view I have of the subject.

Gentlemen

Your arduous & well tried experience, which
is so conspicuously and ably exerted, gives me confidence;
whereas, under other auspices I might have been silenced; in
such hands as yours it will, if useful be duly remembered,
valued & improved; or laid aside if insufficient with a judge-
ment for which I feel the highest esteem, as an individual
who, partaking of the blessing of being born under a British
Government, must be happy to subscribe himself

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very faithful & humble Servant
Ja:s Sowerby

2 Mead Place

29^th Oct.r 1813.

Navy Office 24^th Nov.r 1813

Sir

Having attentively considered the two Reports
from you, respecting the mode of stowing Timber in the Dock
Yards, We desire you will give the necessary instructions to the
Officers of Deptford Dock Yard for the erection of six Stacks
of Timber to be housed over according to your plan; &, we
acquaint you that the Officers of that Yard have had directions
to afford you every assistance in their power in the prosecution
of this business. We are

Sir

Your humble Servants
F. T. Hartwell
R Seppings
H. Legge

Ja.s Sowerby Esq.r
2. Mead Place
Lambeth.

***

To the Commissioners of H. M. Navy.

Honourable Gentlemen

I received your letter of the 24^th instant
noticing your desires regarding stacking of Timber in Deptford
Dock yard. I will pay the earliest possible attention to it &
am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obedient humble Servant
Ja:s Sowerby

25^th Nov.r 1813

2 Mead Place
Lambeth

To the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy

Honourable Gentlemen

Presuming on the evident improvement in the Dock yards (in many respects) and in their agreement with my suggestions and on the judgement of the high abilities that have referr'd to my reports after so long attentively considering them, not forgetting the thanks of the Lords of the Admiralty so handsomely expressed for my Report of the 4^th August regarding the Queen Charlotte &c by which she was saved, & the promising hopes of a thorough & comfortable prevention of the Dryrot, I trouble your Honourable Board with a short memorial laying before you a statement of part of two years attention to a subject of such National consequence that I cannot for a moment forget, and the urgency of which has even interrupted some of my periodical works to the disappointment of my Friends & consequently my own loss. Confident of your consideration according to your wonted wisdom & that I shall be so encouraged for my zeal as that I may persevere in your laudable desires with the best effects, I am

Honourable Gentlemen

Your very obed^t Servant

Ja^s Sowerby

2. Mead Place, Lambeth

A short statement &c.

1812 July 16^th Attended the Honourable Commissioners of His Majesty's Navy according to their request transmitted by M^r Nelson.

17^th Receiv'd directions to proceed to Plymouth as early as possible, to inspect H.M.S. the Queen Charlotte which had recieved considerable injury from the Dryrot, and to advise the prevention of its further progress, if possible; to report thereon & to extend my report to the means of prevention on board H.M. Ships in future.

24^th Proceeded to Plymouth in company with M^r Lukin, [inserted above: Aug 1^st] attended the Dock yard & H.M.S the Queen Charlotte; visited Portsmouth Dock yard in the way & arrived at home Aug^t 2^d being Sunday.

Aug^t 4^th Delivered my report on the Queen Charlotte pointing out the possibility of repairing her & recommending the proper means for that purpose

1813 Jan^y 28^th Answered application from your Honourable Board requesting my opinion of M^r Packers letter stating that he had discovered the method of curing the Dryrot.

Feb^y 19^th Presented my Report on the measures necessary to be taken for the prevention of the evil of Dryrot on It was generally supposed that the Dryrot had rendered this ship incurable & she is now on service, providentially saved by my directions; I need not mention the value of such saving to those conversant with the subject, it would certainly be enough to afford ample indemnification for my past services; and, also upon future ones, reckoning upon other first rates to be assuredly saved & to be saved by such an example properly applied, the amount would be immense.

board H.M.S. in future"

March 21^st Further information given as to the address of the makers of Hygrometers.

Oct^r 25. Attended Commissioner Seppings to Deptford & Woolwich Dock Yards according to the desire of your Honourable Board expressed on the 16^th

Oct^r 29^th Presented my report thereon, pointing out how far my suggestions relating to stacking of Timber could be carried into execution &c.

Navy Office 5^th January 1814.

Sir,

Having had under consideration your letter of
the ___ ultimo, We acquaint you that we have awarded to you
the sum of Two hundred Guineas for the services which you have
rendered, in order to prevent as much as possible the alarming in-
crease of the Dryrot in His Majesty's Ships; & to introduce
an improved mode of seasoning & stowing away Timber: At
the same time we deem it necessary to apprise you that in
awarding this sum we do not attach to you the merit of
having saved the Queen Charlotte by your advices, all the
defective materials having been taken out of that Ship at
Plymouth when she was repaired there.

We are
Sir
Your humble Servants
T. B. Thompson
R. Seppings
H. Legge.

Ja^s Sowerby Esq^r

[Stamp at bottom center: circular stamp reading "BRITISH MUSEUM (NATURAL HISTORY)" around the edge and "26 JUN 1937" in the center]
